meta.title: Material switching playbooks
meta.note: These playbooks are meant to preserve testing discipline during substitutions. They assume the studio is changing one meaningful thing at a time and recording both bucket behaviour and fired results.

whenToUse: the dry glaze layer is cracking or shrinking too much before firing
a matte or high-clay glaze crawls before chemistry changes are even tested
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Calcined kaolin preserves fired chemistry direction well but removes some of the raw clay behaviour that helped suspension and dry adhesion.
playbooks.firstMove: Start with a split rather than a full replacement so the glaze keeps some raw-clay handling.
expectedChemistryShift: fired chemistry stays close if the substitution is done by equivalent fired contribution
raw LOI and drying behaviour change immediately
workflowShift: the glaze may settle differently or feel less brush-friendly
bucket fixes may be needed even if the fired result improves
testSequence: make a control batch with the original kaolin
make split versions rather than jumping straight to all-calcined
compare dry layer quality, then fire only the best-behaving branch
stopIf: the glaze loses too much suspension or application quality
the fired surface begins to dry out because other compensations were ignored

whenToUse: a carbonate-heavy glaze is pinholing or blistering
the studio wants calcium with less gas release
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Wollastonite brings CaO and SiO2 together, so it changes more than the gas burden.
playbooks.firstMove: Treat wollastonite as a chemistry rebuild, not as a direct whiting replacement.
expectedChemistryShift: CO2 release drops
silica contribution rises unless the recipe is recalculated
workflowShift: melt behaviour often gets cleaner
the glaze can stiffen or change gloss if the added silica is ignored
testSequence: build a control batch with the original whiting recipe
make a chemistry-aware wollastonite version
fire both with the same thickness and schedule to see whether the real win came from lower gas or from the silica shift
stopIf: clarity drops because the recipe was never recalculated for the extra silica
the pinhole problem was really a firing schedule issue, not a whiting issue

whenToUse: a legacy recipe depends on Gerstley Borate but supply or consistency is unacceptable
the studio wants a more stable boron route
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: There is no universal one-material replacement because Gerstley Borate contributes boron, calcium, sodium, magnesium, and slurry personality all at once.
playbooks.firstMove: Rebuild the chemistry in parts instead of searching for one magic substitute.
expectedChemistryShift: boron source becomes more repeatable
the old mineral-specific slurry behaviour disappears
workflowShift: the glaze often becomes more predictable in the bucket
the surface can look cleaner but may lose some legacy character
testSequence: capture the original recipe as a control even if it is flawed
rebuild the boron and calcium direction with frits and supporting materials
run line blends around the rebuilt version because the first chemistry match is rarely the last one needed
stopIf: the studio starts accepting a pretty result without checking whether the original intent was actually preserved
the rebuild quietly raises expansion or loses needed magnesium

whenToUse: a recipe is written around North American potash feldspar but the studio has the other common brand
a historical recipe must be recreated more carefully
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Both materials are labeled like ordinary potash feldspars, but the sodium and potassium balance is not identical.
playbooks.firstMove: Run a direct comparison batch before adding other corrections.
expectedChemistryShift: K2O to Na2O balance moves slightly
fit and colour-break behaviour can drift even when the recipe still looks broadly similar
workflowShift: bucket behaviour usually changes less than fired behaviour
this is a subtle substitution that can still matter a lot in stable or colour-critical glazes
testSequence: make one control and one one-material substitution batch
fire on the intended body with the same application thickness
only if needed, tune silica or flux balance after the first comparison
stopIf: the studio changes three materials at once and loses the ability to see what the feldspar did
the recipe is an old archival formula and the test is judged without acknowledging era-specific material drift

whenToUse: the glaze is under-melting at mid fire and a more active feldspathic flux is desired
a studio wants to push a recipe down in temperature or increase melt
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Nepheline syenite is not just a hotter feldspar; it shifts both melt activity and expansion direction.
playbooks.firstMove: Use it as a branch test with careful fit observation rather than as a hidden recipe update.
expectedChemistryShift: Na2O pressure usually rises
melt activity usually rises earlier in the firing
workflowShift: the glaze may become more fluid and brighter
crazing risk often rises before the surface visually warns you
testSequence: substitute only part of the feldspar first
run a fit-ring tile or rim test alongside the visual tiles
compare both melt quality and delayed craze lines after cooling
stopIf: the glaze starts fitting worse even though it looks more mature
the studio mistakes more gloss for better chemistry

whenToUse: a glaze is too fluid or too craze-prone in a 3134-heavy direction
the studio wants a slightly more buffered boron frit path
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: 3195 is not only a boron source change; it also shifts alumina, magnesium, and the overall feel of the melt.
playbooks.firstMove: Compare the two frit directions in the same base recipe before changing anything else.
expectedChemistryShift: melt usually gets a little less aggressive
expansion risk often eases slightly compared with a high-3134 branch
workflowShift: clarity or gloss can shift subtly
a previously dramatic colour host may become calmer
testSequence: build a control using the original 3134 recipe
make a 3195 branch and keep thickness identical
use the same stain or colour host panel if colour behaviour was part of the reason for the change
stopIf: the studio assumes a calmer frit automatically fixed fit or durability
the change hides a firing problem that should have been corrected first

whenToUse: a low-fire clear is too high expansion for the body
the studio wants to tune fit while staying in the same general clear family
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: 3110 and 3249 push low-fire clear behaviour in opposite directions, so the balance affects clarity, melt, and fit at once.
playbooks.firstMove: Adjust the clear family balance rather than adding random silica or talc first.
expectedChemistryShift: Na2O pressure drops as the 3249 branch increases
MgO influence rises and the overall expansion direction drops
workflowShift: the lower-expansion clear may look slightly less brilliant or more restrained
fit usually improves before the glaze becomes visually dramatic
testSequence: keep the rest of the recipe fixed
make a short line blend from the hotter branch toward the lower-expansion branch
check clarity and delayed crazing together rather than separately
stopIf: the studio celebrates a fit correction that actually underfired the surface
the body itself was never fired high enough to judge fit honestly

whenToUse: a legacy barium recipe must be made more conservative for modern studio handling
a surface idea depends on alkaline-earth behaviour but raw soluble barium is not acceptable
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Strontium is a safer workflow choice but it does not recreate barium chemistry perfectly.
playbooks.firstMove: Treat the strontium version as a new branch that must earn its place on its own.
expectedChemistryShift: the surface often becomes cleaner or less idiosyncratic
colour and texture can shift even when the chemistry direction stays related
workflowShift: raw handling safety improves
surface identity may become more ordinary if the original glaze depended heavily on barium-specific behaviour
testSequence: make the original only if the studio is prepared to handle it safely for comparison
test the strontium branch on the same body and schedule
judge not only the target colour but also gloss, fit, and edge behaviour
stopIf: the studio calls the substitution successful just because the colour stayed in the same family
functional claims are made without fresh testing

whenToUse: the studio wants the same copper direction with less carbonate gas
a recipe is pinholing or bubbling and copper is one suspected contributor
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Copper oxide is stronger by weight and changes both LOI and colourant strength at once.
playbooks.firstMove: Reduce the copper amount rather than starting at equal weight.
expectedChemistryShift: copper effect can strengthen even when the number on paper goes down
gassing load drops because the carbonate component is gone
workflowShift: the glaze may heal bubbles better if the rest of the system is sound
the same recipe can over-colour or crawl if the switch is treated casually
testSequence: make a control with copper carbonate
make a reduced copper-oxide branch
compare colour, melt, and surface healing on the same firing
stopIf: the studio keeps increasing copper because the first oxide test looked different
food-surface caution is ignored because the material label changed

whenToUse: a recipe must be rebuilt around oxide instead of carbonate
the studio wants to compare smoother cobalt colour against specklier cobalt response
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Cobalt oxide is stronger and can look different even when the hue family stays blue.
playbooks.firstMove: Use a lower oxide amount and compare directly against the carbonate version.
expectedChemistryShift: blue saturation usually rises at lower weights
surface speckle or texture can appear if the oxide grade is coarser
workflowShift: the glaze can look more forceful or harsher if the loading is not reduced
small measurement errors become even more visible
testSequence: make control and oxide branches only
fire on both smooth and textured tiles if speckle matters
pick the lower-strength result unless the stronger blue is clearly better
stopIf: the studio treats oxide and carbonate as equal-strength forms
subtle blue quality is lost by chasing maximum saturation

whenToUse: a natural-base glaze shrinks, cracks, or crawls in the dry stage
the studio wants the same family with calmer bucket behaviour
playbooks.whyThisIsHard: Roasting keeps the fired chemistry direction but changes weight, LOI, and the way the slurry behaves.
playbooks.firstMove: Blend roasted and raw versions rather than jumping straight to fully roasted.
expectedChemistryShift: fired chemistry direction stays related if roast-loss math is respected
raw shrinkage and gas burden drop
workflowShift: the glaze usually dries more cleanly and shrinks less
suspension and density can change enough that water amount must be revisited
testSequence: make a control with the raw natural base
make split roasted branches
compare dry adhesion, sieving feel, and fired family side by side
stopIf: the studio assumes the roasted version is automatically a better recipe
roast-loss math is ignored and the chemistry drifts unnoticed
